extracted_information: OverlayFS is a union filesystem recommended as a Docker storage driver (`overlay2`) for Linux kernel version 4.0+ (or RHEL/CentOS 3.10.0-514+). It supports `xfs` backing filesystems with `d_type=true` (`ftype=1`). Changing the storage driver makes existing containers and images inaccessible; users must save or push images before switching. The `overlay2` driver combines read-only `lowerdir` (image layers) and a writable `upperdir` (container layer) into a single `merged` view. It natively supports up to 128 lower OverlayFS layers, improving performance for `docker build` and `docker commit` and consuming fewer inodes. Docker manages files/directories in `/var/lib/docker/overlay2`, which should not be manually manipulated.

specifications.kernel_version_prerequisite: Linux kernel 4.0 or higher, or RHEL/CentOS 3.10.0-514 or higher

features.name: Union Filesystem
features.description: Combines multiple directory layers (lowerdir, upperdir) into a single unified view (merged directory).

features.name: Layered Storage
features.description: Docker images consist of read-only layers (`lowerdir`), and containers add a writable layer (`upperdir`) for changes. The `merged` directory provides the unified view.

features.name: Copy-on-Write (CoW)
features.description: Modifications to existing files in the image layer trigger a `copy_up` operation, moving the file to the container's writable `upperdir` before modification. Subsequent writes operate on this copy. This is done at the file level, copying the entire file.

features.name: File Reading Precedence
features.description: When a file exists in both image and container layers, the version in the `upperdir` takes precedence. If only in `lowerdir`, it's read directly. If only in `upperdir`, it's read directly.

features.name: File and Directory Deletion
features.description: Deleting a file creates a `whiteout` file in the `upperdir`, obscuring the original file in the `lowerdir`. Deleting a directory creates an `opaque directory` in the `upperdir`, preventing access to the original directory.

features.name: Page Cache Sharing
features.description: Supports sharing of page cache among multiple containers accessing the same file, making it memory efficient for high-density use cases (e.g., PaaS).

features.name: Inodes Consumption
features.description: Efficient in consuming fewer inodes on the backing filesystem, particularly due to native support for multiple layers.

references.description: Rootless mode documentation for fuse-overlayfs driver

storage_security_implications.layer_isolation: Docker image layers (`lowerdir`) are read-only, ensuring their integrity. Container-specific changes are isolated to the writable `upperdir`. The `copy_up` mechanism and `whiteout`/`opaque directory` operations ensure that a container's modifications or deletions do not affect the base image or other containers using the same image layers.
storage_security_implications.filesystem_security: Docker strongly advises against directly manipulating files or directories within `/var/lib/docker/` as these are managed by Docker itself, implying a controlled and isolated environment for container and image data. The read-only nature of base layers contributes to image immutability from a container's perspective.
storage_security_implications.limitations_and_workarounds: OverlayFS only implements a subset of POSIX standards, which can lead to unexpected behavior for applications. For example, `open(2)` can reference different file descriptors after a `copy_up`, requiring applications to `touch` files or handle this. `rename(2)` for directories is only allowed on the top layer, otherwise it returns `EXDEV`, requiring applications to fall back to a 'copy and unlink' strategy. `yum` users may need `yum-plugin-ovl` or a `touch /var/lib/rpm/*` workaround due to this limitation.
